original_reasoning: Same pair as above in accusative plural. الصيم (compact/hard) vs καταλύσιμος (dissolvable) are antonyms on the same structural axis.

البرص = leprosy; سام أبرص = large gecko (associated with disease/harm). βλαβερός = harmful, pernicious. Leprosy is the paradigmatic harmful disease. The Arabic root carries intrinsic harm, and the Greek adjective means exactly harmful. Strong semantic alignment.
Arabic = coarse, troublesome, oafish person. Greek = harmful, pernicious. Both describe a harmful/troublesome character. Plausible semantic chain from coarse-troublesome to harmful.
Arabic 'that which is strung/arranged' and Greek 'separate' can reflect two sides of handling units: binding versus unbinding.
Arabic faz' = 'panic, fleeing'; Greek = 'having fled'. Plausible conceptual overlap.
Mixed/pasture/wild vs 'mad, furious, rampant' - faint via wild pasturing
Arabic خِطاب is directed speech or exchange, and Greek hold toward or attend can preserve the interactional core.
Arabic slackening or loosening can conceptually meet Greek sleep as a state of relaxation or sinking from tension.
